This market will resolve to the amount of basis points the upper bound of the target federal funds rate is changed by versus the level it was prior to the Federal Reserve's October 2025 meeting.

Howard Schneider and Ann Saphir

Powell in Washington, D.C., U.S., September 17, 2025. REUTERS/Elizabeth Frantz

As expected, the Fed [cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

* Only Miran, who joined Tuesday and is on leave as the head of the White House's Council of Economic Advisers, dissented in favor of a half-percentage-point cut.
* New projections show that two more quarter-percentage-point reductions are anticipated at the remaining two policy meetings this year.
* [Stocks](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/fed-rate-decision-live-updates-stocks-mixed-interest-rate-cut-expectations-2025-09-17/?arena_mid=K1X1HxZf8hcsuBRH4p5h) briefly rose after the decision before turning lower, while the dollar was modestly higher against a basket of major trading partners' currencies.

The cut and projections indicate Fed officials have begun to downplay the risk that the Trump administration's [trade policies](https://www.reuters.com/business/tariffs/) will stoke persistent inflation, and are now more concerned about weakening growth and the likelihood of rising unemployment.

We're wrapping up our live coverage for now, but you can catch the latest [here](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

As Powell's press conference wrapped up, Trump was headed to a state banquet, where he was welcomed by King Charles and congratulated for his "personal commitment to finding solutions to some of the world's most intractable conflicts."

And concerns that Trump's interference with the Fed - through constant jawboning over rates, the appointment of a White House insider to the Fed board and his effort to fire Cook - would yield signs of outsized political influence appear to have been overblown.

Only Miran dissented for a larger rate cut, and the president's other two appointees joined the wider consensus after dissenting just one meeting earlier.

## Fed easing a mixed blessing for rest of the world

15 hours ago

17:38 GMT -3

By![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5f676b3a-8980-43f9-820b-1fb1a1d9db08.png)

Jamie McGeever, a columnist for Reuters

We're about to see a [rare phenomenon in global central banking](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/): The [Fed](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/federal-reserve/) embark on an interest rate-cutting cycle just as many of its peers are winding theirs down.

There have been four large global easing cycles since the euro's launch in 1999, including the current one.

In the previous three, the Fed was either one of the first big central banks to move, as was the case in 2019, or among the most aggressive rate cutters, as was the case in the dotcom bust.

But last year the Fed was relatively slow off the blocks, as sticky inflation and solid growth meant it pulled the trigger after most of its peers.

As a result, the Fed now finds itself playing catch up, especially against the European Central Bank and Bank of Canada, which have cut rates 200 and 225 bps in this cycle, respectively.

Rates futures markets are pricing in around 150 basis points of Fed cuts by the end of next year.

Traders expect only another 40-60 bps over the same period from the BoE, BOC, and Reserve Banks of Australia and New Zealand.

Meanwhile, the ECB and Swiss National Bank are thought to be done, while the Bank of Japan is slowly raising rates, taking its own unique path.

This policy divergence may create some problems beyond U.S. shores.

Read more of this commentary [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/).

## No third mandate, says Powell

16 hours ago

16:34 GMT -3

Powell was asked about Miran's suggestion during his recent confirmation hearing that the Fed has a third mandate-moderate long-term interest rates.

Since 1977, the Fed has been tasked by Congress with a dual mandate: ensure stable prices and maximum employment.

"We always think of it as the dual mandate, maximum employment and price stability, for a long time, because we think moderate long-term interest rates are something that will result from ... low and stable inflation and maximum employment," Powell responded.

"We haven't thought about that for a very long time as a third mandate that requires independent action," he said.

He added, "As far as I'm concerned, there's no thought of considering that we somehow incorporate that in as something in a different way."

## On Trump White House pressure, Powell mostly has no comment

16 hours ago

16:24 GMT -3

By

![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/9eb74fe1-b38d-4125-913d-d977b264d337.jpg)![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5ec09468-2493-49fd-af28-3b1cdd524955.jpg)

Zoe Law and Heather Timmons

FILE PHOTO: Trump and Powell at the White House in Washington, November 2, 2017. REUTERS/Carlos Barria

In these Fed press conferences, Powell is often asked about the pressure from the Trump administration to cut rates or critique that the Fed is not independent.

He mostly has one answer - I'm not going to talk about that.

Wednesday was no exception, despite reporters' best efforts:

**Q:** Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said that the Federal Reserve suffers from mission creep and institutional bloat. He's now calling for an independent review. Would you support an independent review or are you open to any sort of reform in any areas of the Fed?

**A:** I, of course, I'm not going to comment on anything the Secretary says or really any other officer says.

He was also asked about his plan for when his term as chair expires in May.

**Q:** I also wanted to ask if you could share with us the conditions under which you might consider leaving the Fed in May.

**A:** I have nothing new on that for you today.

And he was asked about Trump's push to fire Lisa Cook:

**Q:** So do you see the court case around Lisa Cook as being related to questions about independence?

**A:** You know, I see it as a court case, that it would be inappropriate for me ... to comment on.

## Powell: 'We're doing our work exactly as we always have'

17 hours ago

16:08 GMT -3

By![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5b6b3057-f191-4373-a0dd-285210820735.jpg)

Vanessa Balintec

Powell in Washington, D.C., September 17, 2025. REUTERS/Elizabeth Frantz

Powell was again asked about the Fed's independence.

Asked how he can reassure markets that the Fed is making decisions based on economic outlook rather than political considerations, Powell said that the Fed's decision is based on data.

"It's deeply in our culture to do our work based on the incoming data and never consider anything else. That's just everybody who's at the Fed really feels strongly about that way," said Powell.

"We don't frame these questions at all or see them in terms of political outcomes. I think when you get to another part of Washington, everything is seen through the lens of does it help or hurt this political party, this politician? ... And I think people find it hard to believe that that's just not at all the way we think about things at the Fed. We're taking a longer perspective."

"We're doing our work exactly as we always have now. And people are, they're making their arguments. And we're having a really great discussion around these challenging issues."

## How analysts link immigration and job growth

17 hours ago

16:06 GMT -3

By

![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)

Lucia Mutikani and Mike Dolan, Markets Editor-at-Large

Powell has suggested that the U.S.' recent weakness in employment could be linked to immigration.

It is a scenario some analysts have warned of in recent months.

THE STATISTICS

Nonfarm payrolls increased by only 22,000 jobs [last month](https://www.reuters.com/business/us-unemployment-rate-near-4-year-high-labor-market-hits-stall-speed-2025-09-05/) after rising by an upwardly revised 79,000 in July, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) said. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast payrolls would rise by 75,000 jobs after a previously reported gain of 73,000 in July.

Revisions to the establishment survey data also showed payrolls declined by 13,000 jobs in June, the first drop since December 2020, rather than rising by 14,000, as had been reported last month.

IMMIGRATION SHIFTS

The flow of migrant workers into the U.S. has effectively halted over the past year. The pace was already slowing sharply before the election but has ground to a near halt along with the [rise in deportations](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/immigration-raids-los-angeles-hit-small-business-owners-its-worse-than-covid-2025-06-17/) this year. Couple that with the steadily aging population of existing workers, and it looks like a labor crunch could be on the horizon, as Reuters columnist Mike Dolan [wrote in June](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/us-migrant-halt-may-wipe-potential-job-growth-2025-06-24/).

PREDICTIONS

Economists at Barclays tracking these trends reckon that "potential" non-farm private payrolls growth - or the level of extra jobs that can be created without leading to worker shortages - could fall to less than 10,000 per month by the end of next year from more than 100,000 today.

They estimate that potential job growth will fall to about 60,000 within the next six months, slowing potential economic growth to only 1.4-1.6% year on- ear through next year from just over 2% now.

Read more Reuters commentary on jobs and immigration [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/us-migrant-halt-may-wipe-potential-job-growth-2025-06-24/) and [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/europe/trump-immigration-crackdown-creates-jobs-distortions-fed-headaches-mcgeever-2025-06-10/).

Powell said some of the more dire inflationary scenarios facing the economy have faded.

"Really since April, to me, the risks of higher and more persistent inflation have probably become a little less, and that's partly because the [labor market](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/fed-rate-decision-live-updates-stocks-mixed-interest-rate-cut-expectations-2025-09-17/?arena_mid=HvPjMqQZMmPVbvDDrWVb) has softened, GDP growth has slowed," Powell said.

"There wasn't widespread support at all for a 50-basis-point cut today," Powell told reporters.

## Powell says immigration, not tariffs weighing on employment

15:47 GMT -3

By

![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/fce1174e-3bc7-43a2-8d5c-007017d008a8.png)![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5b6b3057-f191-4373-a0dd-285210820735.jpg)

Howard Schneider and Vanessa Balintec

Powell in Washington, D.C., September 17, 2025. REUTERS/Elizabeth Frantz

Powell was asked: "Do you see that as a possible outcome here, that tariffs are the reason we're seeing some slowing, particularly in the labor market, rather than in inflation?"

He answered:

"It's certainly possible," he said.

"We have begun to see goods prices showing through into higher inflation. And actually the increase in goods prices accounts for most of the increase in inflation or perhaps all of the increase in inflation over the course of this year.

"I would say if you're looking at why employment is doing what it's doing, it's much more about the change in immigration."

He added, "So the supply of workers has obviously come way down. There's very little growth, if any, in the supply of workers. And at the same time, demand for workers has also come down quite sharply."

You are an expert prediction-market analyst. You have been given an amount of USD $1.0 to allocate on the following event from the prediction market Polymarket.

<event_details>
- Date: September 18, 2025
- Title: Fed decision in October?
- Description: The FED interest rates are defined in this market by the upper bound of the target federal funds range. The decisions on the target federal fund range are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings.

This market will resolve to the amount of basis points the upper bound of the target federal funds rate is changed by versus the level it was prior to the Federal Reserve's October 2025 meeting.

Summary

## What's happening?

* [Federal Reserve](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/federal-reserve/) board met as U.S. President [Donald Trump](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/) threatens independence
* Rates cut by quarter percentage point to 4.00%-4.25%
* Chair Jerome Powell says inflation risks tilt upside
* Markets expect further cuts in 2025
* Trump appointee Stephen Miran cast lone dissent
* Governor Lisa Cook [remains](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/lisa-cook-live-court-ruling-trump-case-could-impact-fed-meeting-2025-09-15/) despite Trump's attempt to fire her

## What happened Wednesday

14 hours ago

18:13 GMT -3

By

![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/fce1174e-3bc7-43a2-8d5c-007017d008a8.png)![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/98b080a3-6c79-418c-b01b-7b319e28fd90.jpeg)

Howard Schneider and Ann Saphir

Powell in Washington, D.C., U.S., September 17, 2025. REUTERS/Elizabeth Frantz

As expected, the Fed [cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

* Only Miran, who joined Tuesday and is on leave as the head of the White House's Council of Economic Advisers, dissented in favor of a half-percentage-point cut.
* New projections show that two more quarter-percentage-point reductions are anticipated at the remaining two policy meetings this year.
* [Stocks](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/fed-rate-decision-live-updates-stocks-mixed-interest-rate-cut-expectations-2025-09-17/?arena_mid=K1X1HxZf8hcsuBRH4p5h) briefly rose after the decision before turning lower, while the dollar was modestly higher against a basket of major trading partners' currencies.

The cut and projections indicate Fed officials have begun to downplay the risk that the Trump administration's [trade policies](https://www.reuters.com/business/tariffs/) will stoke persistent inflation, and are now more concerned about weakening growth and the likelihood of rising unemployment.

We're wrapping up our live coverage for now, but you can catch the latest [here](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

## Wait, where's Trump?

15 hours ago

17:56 GMT -3

By

![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5ec09468-2493-49fd-af28-3b1cdd524955.jpg)![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/ea2034ae-1cd5-41cf-8c70-8fdba539596a.jpeg)

Heather Timmons and Dan Burns

King Charles delivers his speech at Windsor Castle, Berkshire, September 17, 2025. Aaron Chown/Pool via REUTERS

The U.S. President has been publicly pressuring the Fed and Powell to cut rates for months.

But his Truth Social account was quiet after Wednesday's meeting and rate cut.

Trump has been on a [state visit in Britain](https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/uk-embraces-trump-with-royal-pomp-historic-state-visit-2025-09-17/), where he has been greeted by royal pomp and protests.

As Powell's press conference wrapped up, Trump was headed to a state banquet, where he was welcomed by King Charles and congratulated for his "personal commitment to finding solutions to some of the world's most intractable conflicts."

And concerns that Trump's interference with the Fed - through constant jawboning over rates, the appointment of a White House insider to the Fed board and his effort to fire Cook - would yield signs of outsized political influence appear to have been overblown.

Only Miran dissented for a larger rate cut, and the president's other two appointees joined the wider consensus after dissenting just one meeting earlier.

## Fed easing a mixed blessing for rest of the world

15 hours ago

17:38 GMT -3

By![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5f676b3a-8980-43f9-820b-1fb1a1d9db08.png)

Jamie McGeever, a columnist for Reuters

We're about to see a [rare phenomenon in global central banking](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/): The [Fed](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/federal-reserve/) embark on an interest rate-cutting cycle just as many of its peers are winding theirs down.

There have been four large global easing cycles since the euro's launch in 1999, including the current one.

In the previous three, the Fed was either one of the first big central banks to move, as was the case in 2019, or among the most aggressive rate cutters, as was the case in the dotcom bust.

But last year the Fed was relatively slow off the blocks, as sticky inflation and solid growth meant it pulled the trigger after most of its peers.

As a result, the Fed now finds itself playing catch up, especially against the European Central Bank and Bank of Canada, which have cut rates 200 and 225 bps in this cycle, respectively.

Rates futures markets are pricing in around 150 basis points of Fed cuts by the end of next year.

Traders expect only another 40-60 bps over the same period from the BoE, BOC, and Reserve Banks of Australia and New Zealand.

Meanwhile, the ECB and Swiss National Bank are thought to be done, while the Bank of Japan is slowly raising rates, taking its own unique path.

This policy divergence may create some problems beyond U.S. shores.

Read more of this commentary [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/).

## How analysts link immigration and job growth

17 hours ago

16:06 GMT -3

By

![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)

Lucia Mutikani and Mike Dolan, Markets Editor-at-Large

Powell has suggested that the U.S.' recent weakness in employment could be linked to immigration.

It is a scenario some analysts have warned of in recent months.

THE STATISTICS

Nonfarm payrolls increased by only 22,000 jobs [last month](https://www.reuters.com/business/us-unemployment-rate-near-4-year-high-labor-market-hits-stall-speed-2025-09-05/) after rising by an upwardly revised 79,000 in July, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) said. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast payrolls would rise by 75,000 jobs after a previously reported gain of 73,000 in July.

Revisions to the establishment survey data also showed payrolls declined by 13,000 jobs in June, the first drop since December 2020, rather than rising by 14,000, as had been reported last month.

IMMIGRATION SHIFTS

The flow of migrant workers into the U.S. has effectively halted over the past year. The pace was already slowing sharply before the election but has ground to a near halt along with the [rise in deportations](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/immigration-raids-los-angeles-hit-small-business-owners-its-worse-than-covid-2025-06-17/) this year. Couple that with the steadily aging population of existing workers, and it looks like a labor crunch could be on the horizon, as Reuters columnist Mike Dolan [wrote in June](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/us-migrant-halt-may-wipe-potential-job-growth-2025-06-24/).

PREDICTIONS

Economists at Barclays tracking these trends reckon that "potential" non-farm private payrolls growth - or the level of extra jobs that can be created without leading to worker shortages - could fall to less than 10,000 per month by the end of next year from more than 100,000 today.

They estimate that potential job growth will fall to about 60,000 within the next six months, slowing potential economic growth to only 1.4-1.6% year on- ear through next year from just over 2% now.

Read more Reuters commentary on jobs and immigration [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/us-migrant-halt-may-wipe-potential-job-growth-2025-06-24/) and [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/europe/trump-immigration-crackdown-creates-jobs-distortions-fed-headaches-mcgeever-2025-06-10/).

Summary

## What's happening?

* [Federal Reserve](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/federal-reserve/) board met as U.S. President [Donald Trump](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/) threatens independence
* Rates cut by quarter percentage point to 4.00%-4.25%
* Chair Jerome Powell says inflation risks tilt upside
* Markets expect further cuts in 2025
* Trump appointee Stephen Miran cast lone dissent
* Governor Lisa Cook [remains](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/lisa-cook-live-court-ruling-trump-case-could-impact-fed-meeting-2025-09-15/) despite Trump's attempt to fire her

## What happened Wednesday

14 hours ago

18:13 GMT -3

By

![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/fce1174e-3bc7-43a2-8d5c-007017d008a8.png)![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/98b080a3-6c79-418c-b01b-7b319e28fd90.jpeg)

Howard Schneider and Ann Saphir

Powell in Washington, D.C., U.S., September 17, 2025. REUTERS/Elizabeth Frantz

As expected, the Fed [cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

* Only Miran, who joined Tuesday and is on leave as the head of the White House's Council of Economic Advisers, dissented in favor of a half-percentage-point cut.
* New projections show that two more quarter-percentage-point reductions are anticipated at the remaining two policy meetings this year.
* [Stocks](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/fed-rate-decision-live-updates-stocks-mixed-interest-rate-cut-expectations-2025-09-17/?arena_mid=K1X1HxZf8hcsuBRH4p5h) briefly rose after the decision before turning lower, while the dollar was modestly higher against a basket of major trading partners' currencies.

The cut and projections indicate Fed officials have begun to downplay the risk that the Trump administration's [trade policies](https://www.reuters.com/business/tariffs/) will stoke persistent inflation, and are now more concerned about weakening growth and the likelihood of rising unemployment.

We're wrapping up our live coverage for now, but you can catch the latest [here](https://www.reuters.com/business/fed-delivers-normal-sized-rate-cut-sees-steady-pace-further-reductions-miran-2025-09-17/).

## Fed easing a mixed blessing for rest of the world

15 hours ago

17:38 GMT -3

By![](https://cdn.getarena.im/authors/5f676b3a-8980-43f9-820b-1fb1a1d9db08.png)

Jamie McGeever, a columnist for Reuters

We're about to see a [rare phenomenon in global central banking](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/): The [Fed](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/federal-reserve/) embark on an interest rate-cutting cycle just as many of its peers are winding theirs down.

There have been four large global easing cycles since the euro's launch in 1999, including the current one.

In the previous three, the Fed was either one of the first big central banks to move, as was the case in 2019, or among the most aggressive rate cutters, as was the case in the dotcom bust.

But last year the Fed was relatively slow off the blocks, as sticky inflation and solid growth meant it pulled the trigger after most of its peers.

As a result, the Fed now finds itself playing catch up, especially against the European Central Bank and Bank of Canada, which have cut rates 200 and 225 bps in this cycle, respectively.

Rates futures markets are pricing in around 150 basis points of Fed cuts by the end of next year.

Traders expect only another 40-60 bps over the same period from the BoE, BOC, and Reserve Banks of Australia and New Zealand.

Meanwhile, the ECB and Swiss National Bank are thought to be done, while the Bank of Japan is slowly raising rates, taking its own unique path.

This policy divergence may create some problems beyond U.S. shores.

Read more of this commentary [here](https://www.reuters.com/markets/funds/fed-easing-mixed-blessing-rest-world-2025-09-17/).

## How analysts link immigration and job growth

17 hours ago

16:06 GMT -3

By

![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)![](https://static.arena.im/v1/accounts/646668b749c07a9471a2426d/avatar.jpg)

Lucia Mutikani and Mike Dolan, Markets Editor-at-Large

Powell has suggested that the U.S.' recent weakness in employment could be linked to immigration.

It is a scenario some analysts have warned of in recent months.

THE STATISTICS

Nonfarm payrolls increased by only 22,000 jobs [last month](https://www.reuters.com/business/us-unemployment-rate-near-4-year-high-labor-market-hits-stall-speed-2025-09-05/) after rising by an upwardly revised 79,000 in July, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) said. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast payrolls would rise by 75,000 jobs after a previously reported gain of 73,000 in July.

Revisions to the establishment survey data also showed payrolls declined by 13,000 jobs in June, the first drop since December 2020, rather than rising by 14,000, as had been reported last month.

IMMIGRATION SHIFTS

The flow of migrant workers into the U.S. has effectively halted over the past year. The pace was already slowing sharply before the election but has ground to a near halt along with the [rise in deportations](https://www.reuters.com/world/us/immigration-raids-los-angeles-hit-small-business-owners-its-worse-than-covid-2025-06-17/) this year. Couple that with the steadily aging population of existing workers, and it looks like a labor crunch could be on the horizon, as Reuters columnist Mike Dolan [wrote in June](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/us-migrant-halt-may-wipe-potential-job-growth-2025-06-24/).

PREDICTIONS

Economists at Barclays tracking these trends reckon that "potential" non-farm private payrolls growth - or the level of extra jobs that can be created without leading to worker shortages - could fall to less than 10,000 per month by the end of next year from more than 100,000 today.

They estimate that potential job growth will fall to about 60,000 within the next six months, slowing potential economic growth to only 1.4-1.6% year on- ear through next year from just over 2% now.

market_decisions.rationale: Recent Reuters coverage confirms the Fed cut rates by 25 bps on September 17 and signals two more quarter-point cuts this year. Fed futures show an 86% probability of a cut at the October meeting. The market price for a 25 bps cut is 0.785, implying a 78.5% chance. Given the strong forward guidance and futures data, the true probability is likely nearer 85‑90%. This suggests the market is undervaluing the cut outcome. I have high confidence because the data comes from multiple sources (official Fed statement, futures, and credible news).

A 50+ bps cut would require a half‑percentage‑point move, which the Fed indicated was not broadly supported (only one dissenting vote for a 50 bps cut). Futures and the Fed’s own language suggest only modest easing. The market price of 0.0735 implies a 7.35% chance. I estimate the true probability around 10‑12%, slightly higher but still low. The market is not hugely mispriced, but a small short position captures the slight over‑pricing on the large cut.

No change is heavily out of favor after the recent 25 bps cut and forward guidance for further easing. The market price of 0.145 (14.5%) likely overstates the probability; I estimate ~10% chance of no change. The mispricing is modest, and with limited capital I prefer not to allocate further.

An increase in rates after a recent cut is extremely unlikely. Market price of 0.0075 (0.75%) already reflects a very low probability. I estimate perhaps 1–2% chance, so the market may slightly underprice the increase, but the edge is tiny and confidence moderate. No bet allocated.
